title: Daemon liveness is inferred from socket-file existence, so stale sockets break commands instead of recovering

evidence.quote: if (!existsSync(socketPath())) ok({ daemon:{ running:false }, windows:[] }); else ok(await request('list', { includeClosed:o.includeClosed }, false));

reasoning: Both autostart and `list` use `existsSync(socketPath())` as the liveness check. If the daemon crashes or is killed and leaves `daemon.sock` behind, `open`/`send`/etc. will skip `ensureDaemon()`, `list` will skip the `running:false` fallback, and every command will fail on connect instead of restarting or reporting a stopped daemon. Because the socket path is stable, one stale file is enough to wedge the CLI until manual cleanup.
reproduction: Start the daemon, kill it uncleanly so `daemon.sock` remains, then run `glimpse list` or `glimpse open ...`. The command fails on socket connection instead of recovering or reporting `running:false`.
recommendation: Use an actual ping/connect probe for liveness, not file existence. On connection errors such as `ENOENT`/`ECONNREFUSED`, remove the stale socket if appropriate and retry through `ensureDaemon()` before surfacing failure.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: Included tests only cover duration parsing, event queue behavior, and loopback detection. There is no daemon crash-recovery or stale-socket integration test.
suggestedRegressionTest: Create a stale socket scenario, then assert both `request('open')` and `glimpse list` recover by starting a new daemon or return a clean `running:false` result.
minimumFixScope: src/ipc/client.ts and the `list` command path in src/cli.ts
